next_best_action: Investigate VPN gateway logs for connection drops correlating with the reported timeframe (since March 5th maintenance window) and check GlobalProtect client logs on the user's Dell laptop for disconnect reasons.

Request the user run a GlobalProtect diagnostic report (Troubleshooting > Logs > Export) and submit it
Verify the WiFi signal strength and stability in Building C, 4th floor, as intermittent WiFi could cause VPN drops
Review VPN session timeout and keepalive settings that may have been modified during maintenance
If the issue is isolated to specific users or locations, investigate network infrastructure on that floor

next_best_action: Retrieve the exact BSOD stop code from Windows Event Viewer (System log, source BugCheck) and run hardware diagnostics to check for failing storage or RAM, given the reported KERNEL_DATA_INPAGE_ERROR pattern.
remediation_steps: Remote into the machine (or request user access) to check Event Viewer > Windows Logs > System for BugCheck events and capture the exact stop code and parameters
Run Dell built-in diagnostics (F12 at boot > Diagnostics) focusing on hard drive and memory tests
Check SMART status of the storage drive using CrystalDiskInfo or Dell SupportAssist
Review Windows Reliability Monitor for the pattern of crashes over the past month
If KERNEL_DATA_INPAGE_ERROR is confirmed, prioritize storage drive replacement as this typically indicates a failing disk
Ensure user's critical files are backed up immediately given the recurring nature of crashes
If hardware tests pass, check for recent driver updates or Windows updates that may correlate with crash timeline

Restore the user to the correct SharePoint permissions group (likely the Legal Department Members group)
Confirm with the user that access is restored and they can retrieve the needed case files
Investigate root cause of permission removal to prevent recurrence

next_best_action: Check the Exchange transport rules and SMTP relay configuration for the 550 5.7.1 error, and verify the user's Outlook profile is not corrupted by testing with Outlook Web App (OWA) to isolate client vs. server issue.
remediation_steps: Ask the user to test sending an email via Outlook Web App (https://outlook.office365.com) to determine if the issue is client-side or server-side
Check Exchange Online message trace for the user's failed send attempts to capture the actual NDR details
Verify the user's mailbox send permissions and any recent changes to transport rules or DLP policies that could cause a 550 5.7.1 rejection
Determine the Outlook client version and check for pending updates that may fix encoding-related display bugs
If OWA works fine, repair the local Outlook profile or create a new one to resolve client-side encoding corruption
Check if the garbled character display is related to a language pack or regional settings change on the user's workstation
Confirm that the colleague Käthe Müller's issue is identical to rule out a broader mail flow problem

next_best_action: Immediately engage the on-call DBA team to investigate the production PostgreSQL database servers (db-prod-east-01 and db-prod-east-02) for connectivity and health, as both primary and failover are unreachable causing a complete CRM application outage.
remediation_steps: Page the on-call DBA team immediately for P1 production database outage
Verify network connectivity to db-prod-east-01.contoso.internal:5432 and db-prod-east-02.contoso.internal:5432 from the application servers
Check the PostgreSQL server status on both primary and failover nodes (systemctl status postgresql, pg_isready)
Review database server system resources (CPU, memory, disk I/O, disk space) for saturation
Check for any infrastructure changes, maintenance windows, or firewall rule modifications that coincide with the 06:30 UTC outage start
If database servers are unreachable, coordinate with Network Operations to check network path and firewall rules between application and database tiers
Once connectivity is restored, monitor the connection pool recovery and verify the circuit breaker resets
Prepare incident post-mortem documenting the outage timeline, root cause, and preventive measures

next_best_action: Dispatch a technician to the 2nd floor near the meeting room to physically inspect the printer, identify the make and model, diagnose the blinking orange light indicator, and check the network connection.
remediation_steps: Dispatch a technician to the 2nd floor to physically inspect the printer and identify the exact make, model, and asset tag
Check the printer's network connectivity - verify the Ethernet cable is properly seated and the network port on the wall is active
Interpret the blinking orange light pattern using the printer manufacturer's diagnostic guide
Check the printer's IP configuration and verify it has a valid IP address on the correct VLAN
Verify the print server or print queue shows the printer and check for any stuck jobs
Power cycle the printer completely (unplug for 30 seconds) and allow it to reinitialize
If the printer reconnects, push a test print from one of the affected workstations to confirm resolution
Update the printer asset record with current configuration details

Provide the TAP to the user securely and instruct her to log in at https://aka.ms/mfasetup
Guide the user through re-registering Microsoft Authenticator as her MFA method

Document the root cause to prevent recurrence

next_best_action: Run network performance diagnostics between the user's workstation and the file server (\\fileserver\shared\projects) to identify the bottleneck causing large file transfers to drop to 50 KB/s.
remediation_steps: Identify the user's network location (building, floor, switch port) and the file server's location to map the network path
Run iperf3 between the user's workstation and the file server to measure raw network throughput independent of SMB
Check the NIC settings on the user's Intel I219-V adapter for auto-negotiation issues, duplex mismatches, or driver problems
Review the switch port statistics for errors, CRC failures, or packet drops on both the user's port and the file server's port
Check if SMB signing or encryption policies have recently changed, as these can significantly impact large file transfer performance
Test large file transfer from another workstation on the same network segment to isolate whether the issue is user-specific or network-wide
Review file server performance metrics (disk I/O, CPU, memory) during transfer attempts to rule out server-side bottleneck

next_best_action: Analyze Microsoft Teams call quality dashboard (CQD) data for the affected users since March 13th to identify the root cause of the consistent ~10 minute call disconnections, focusing on network quality metrics and QoS policy effectiveness.
remediation_steps: Access the Microsoft Teams Call Quality Dashboard (CQD) and pull call records for Gloria Reeves, Nathan Cross, and Deepa Krishnan since March 13th
Analyze call drop patterns - confirm the ~10 minute threshold and identify common network metrics at the point of disconnection
Review the QoS policy changes applied on March 16th night to verify they were correctly implemented and are not inadvertently causing the issue
Check Teams client versions on affected users' devices and ensure they are up to date
Review network firewall and proxy logs for any session timeout policies that could be terminating long-duration real-time media streams at the 10-minute mark
Test with a controlled Teams call from an affected user's workstation while monitoring network traffic to capture the exact disconnect sequence
Check if UDP ports 3478-3481 are consistently available or if a firewall is resetting connections after a timeout period
Verify that the issue is limited to the reported users or if it's affecting a wider group by checking CQD for organization-wide call reliability trends

next_best_action: Identify the docking station make/model and the firmware version that was applied, then check for known issues with the updated firmware regarding display output and attempt a firmware rollback if a known bug is confirmed.
remediation_steps: Identify the exact docking station model and the firmware version that was updated yesterday
Check the docking station manufacturer's release notes for the firmware update for any known display detection issues
Verify the monitor connection type (HDMI, DisplayPort, USB-C) and test with alternative ports on the docking station
Check Device Manager for any display adapter errors or warning indicators after the firmware update
Try connecting the monitor directly to the laptop (bypassing the dock) to confirm the monitor and laptop display output are working
If the issue is confirmed to be firmware-related, roll back the docking station firmware to the previous version
If rollback is not possible, check for a hotfix firmware release from the manufacturer
Test with a known-working docking station to isolate whether the issue is specific to this unit

remediation_steps: Connect to fs-corp-03 and verify current disk space on D: drive (847 MB free of 4 TB as reported)
Inventory the D:\Backups\Archive directory to identify all .bak files, their sizes, and dates, particularly the 2019 files consuming ~1.5 TB
Identify the scheduled task or backup job that is writing to D:\Backups\Archive and determine its owner and purpose
Coordinate with the backup job owner to confirm that the 2019 .bak files are no longer needed and can be safely removed
Delete or archive the confirmed stale backup files to reclaim disk space immediately
Implement a retention policy on the backup job to automatically purge files older than the agreed retention period
Set up disk space monitoring alerts on fs-corp-03 to trigger at 85% and 90% thresholds to provide early warning
Communicate to the affected users that the disk space issue has been resolved
